title: A simplified method to approximate a ROC curve with a Bézier curve to calculate likelihood ratios of quantitative test results
author: Fierz

steps.title: Method
steps.instruction: Receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis provides the LR as the slope of the tangent to the ROC curve at the point corresponding to the test result. A distribution-independent method has recently been described to calculate these slopes by adjusting Bézier curves to the ROC that are defined by tangents to a curve [1] (Fierz, 2018). The mathematical basis of Bézier curves are Bernstein polynomials of degree n defined byBi,n(t)=(ni)ti(1−t)n−iwithtrangingfrom0to1.

steps.title: Method
steps.instruction: For the purpose here, we make use of cubic Bézier curves defined byB(t)=(1−t)3P0+3t(1−t)2P1+3t2(1−t)P2+t3P3

steps.title: Method
steps.instruction: (Fig. 1).Fig. 1Principle of constructing cubic Bézier curves. First, the lines between the control points P0, P1, P2, and P3 are divided by the ratio t leading to T1, T2, and T3. Second, the lines between T1, T2, and T3 are again divided by the ratio t leading to T4, and T5. Third, the line between T4, and T5 is again divided by the ratio t leading to B(t) on the Bézier curve. The line between T4, and T5 is the tangent to B(t).Pierre Bézier (1910–1999) was a French engineer who developed a method of producing computer-driven curves to be used in the design of automobiles at Renault, which came to be known as Bézier curves.Fig 1

steps.title: Method
steps.instruction: Here, a simplified method to adjust a Bézier curve to a ROC curve is presented. The procedure is based on a simplified method for the shape control of cubic Bézier curves [2]. In principle, the shape of the curve is defined by the two endpoints (P0 and P3) with the corresponding tangents to them and a shape control point B(t)=S(u) on the curve (Fig. 2).Fig. 2Shape definition of the Bézier curve. Apart from the endpoints P0 and P3, and the shape point S the shape of the Bézier curve is defined by the two tangents at the endpoints.Fig 2

steps.title: Procedure
steps.instruction: Step1: Definition of the variable t of the Bézier curve

steps.title: Procedure
steps.instruction: The variable t of the Bernstein polynomial has to be introduced and is defined here by txy = (x + y)/2 of the empirical ROC points.Step 2: Selection of the endpoints of the Bézier curve (Fig. 2)

steps.title: Procedure
steps.instruction: The endpoints of the Bézier curve P0=(x0, y0) and P3=(x3, y3) have to be chosen. Subsequently, the range of t has to be adjusted to the range from 0 to 1 as follows: txy = ((x-x0)/(x3-x0)+(y-y0)/y3-y0))/2Step 3: Selection of the shape point of the Bézier curve (Fig. 2)

steps.title: Procedure
steps.instruction: A shape point S(u) has to be chosen. It is recommended here that from the empirical ROC points the one which is closest to the upper left corner is used. This corresponds to the maximal Youden index (J=Se+Sp-1). The t value at that point is denoted u.Step 4: Selection of tangent vectors at the endpoints of the Bézier curve (Fig. 2)

steps.title: Procedure
steps.instruction: Apart from the endpoints P0 and P3, and the shape point S the shape of the Bézier curve is defined by the two tangents at the endpoints:

steps.title: Procedure
steps.instruction: T0 = (g0, h0) and T3 = (g3, h3) are the tangents vectors at P0 and P3 respectively.

steps.title: Procedure
steps.instruction: To start with it is recommended to set

steps.title: Procedure
steps.instruction: T3 = (1, 0) (horizontal line) as in an ideal ROC curve.

steps.title: Procedure
steps.instruction: Later, g0 and h3 can be adjusted to reach an optimal fit of the Bézier curve to the empirical ROC points.

steps.title: Procedure
steps.instruction: Step 5: Calculation of P1 and P2 of the Bézier curve (Fig. 3)Fig. 3Calculation of P1 and P2. P1 and P2 of the Bézier curve can be calculated according to (Yang & Huang, 1993).Fig 3

steps.title: Procedure
steps.instruction: P1 and P2 of the Bézier curve can be calculated according to (Yang & Huang, 1993) with two shape parameters α and β and the following relations:P1=(α/3)T0+P0,andP2=(β/3)T3+P3α=|(xu−a*x0−b*x3)g3(yu−a*y0−b*y3)h3|u*(1−u)2*Δandβ=|(xu−a*x0−b*x3)g0(yu−a*y0−b*y3)h0|u2*(1−u)*Δwhere the notation | | denotes the determinant operator

steps.title: Procedure
steps.instruction: witha=(1−u)2*(1+2u),b=u2*(3−2u)andΔ=|g0g3h0h3|Step 6: Calculation of the tangent slopes (Fig. 4)Fig. 4With P0,1,2,3 being established in this way, the slopes of the tangents, i.e. the LR(t), can be calculated for all t.Fig 4

steps.title: Procedure
steps.instruction: With P0,1,2,3 being established in this way, the slopes of the tangents, i.e. the LR(t), can be calculated for all t withLR(t)=P1y*(1−2t)−(P1y−P2y)*(2t−3t2)+P3y*t2−P0y*(1−t)2P1x*(1−2t)−(P1x−P2x)*(2t−3t2)+P3x*t2−P0x*(1−t)2
